content.full_text:
September 23, 1993

Kevin G. Ennis
District Counsel
Richards, Watson & Gershon
Thirty-eighth Floor
333' South Hope Street
Los Angeles, CA 90071-1469

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-341

Dear Mr. Ennis:

As the General Counsel for the Green Valley County Water
District ("district"), you have requested advice on behalf of
Mr. Fred Cannon, a member of the board of directors, regarding his
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may Mr. Cannon participate in the district
decision to lift the moratorium on the issuance of fire flow
certificates since he has an ownership interest in real property
which will be affected?

2. Does the "public generally" exception apply to
Mr. Cannon?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Mr. Cannon may not participate in the district decision
to lift the moratorium on the issuance of fire flow certificates
since there will be a material financial effect on the real
property in which he has an ownership interest.

2. The "public generally" exception does not apply to
Mr. Cannon.

_______________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-341
Page 2


FACTS

In July 1990, the district adopted a resolution declaring a
water shortage emergency. Based upon that declaration, the
district imposed a moratorium on the issuance of both fire flow
certificates and new water meter connections.

The district is currently considering whether to adopt a
resolution which would lift that portion of the district's
moratorium which precludes the approval of fire flow certificates.
The district is not presently considering release of the portion
of the moratorium which precludes the issuance of new water meter
connections.

A fire flow certificate ("certificate") is a form required to
be signed by a water purveyor, such as the district, when a person
applies for a building permit from the County of Los Angeles. The
certificate form, if signed by a district official, is a statement
that water pipes and hydrants are within a specified distance from
the property that is the subject of the building permit
application. The certificate also is a statement that sufficient
"fire flow" exists; that is, that the district has sufficient
water supply and capacity to suppress a fire on the property in
the event of an emergency. The certificate does not provide that
the proposed residence will be served with domestic water from the
district. Domestic water connections to properties are provided
by water meters; the district currently has a moratorium on the
issuance of new water meter connections.

The district contains approximately 1,800 lots and
approximately 420 residences. Approximately 405 of these
residences are connected to the district's water system by water
meters. The remaining fifteen residences are connected to
independent and private well and water tank systems. Many of the
residences in the district are constructed on more than one lot
and/or are surrounded by lots that are covenanted together.

There are a significant number of vacant lots that can be
developed in the district. Of these vacant lots, only thirty-
three water meters. The majority of vacant lots with
development potential, estimated to be several hundred in number,
are currently not served by a water meter.

Some of the owners of both types of property (vacant with
meters and vacant without meters) would like to start building on
their property and would like to have the district lift the
moratorium on the issuance of certificates so they can obtain a
building permit. If the property owner does not have a water
meter, the County Planning Department could issue a building
permit if the applicant either obtains approval from the district
for a water meter, builds a well on the site or constructs a water
tank to be served with water trucked in from outside the district.

File No. A-93-341
Page 3


     Since the district currently has a moratorium on the issuance
of new water meters, applicants for water meters were placed on a
long waiting list for a one water meter a month allocation.
Consequently, if the moratorium on fire flow certificates is
lifted, property owners without a water meter who desire to
develop their property would likely have to undergo the first two
additional steps and potential impediments in showing the
availability of water to the site before building could commence.
On the other hand, property owners with a water meter would be
likely to obtain a building permit more quickly.

     District Board Member Cannon is a one-quarter owner of a five
acre parcel of vacant land in the district. The parcel is one of
the thirty-three vacant lots in the district served by a water
meter. Board Member Cannon estimates that the property is worth
approximately $120,000 now and would be worth approximately
$150,000 if a certificate could be issued so that a structure
could be built on the property.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participate in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know the official has a financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family,
or on:

               (b) Any real property in which the public
               official has a direct or indirect interest
               worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                              Section 87103(b).

     Mr. Cannon is a one-quarter owner of a five acre parcel of
vacant land which is currently valued at $120,000. Therefore, he
has an interest in real property worth in excess of $1,000.
Accordingly, Mr. Cannon may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally, on his real property interest.

Foreseeability

     Generally, an effect of a decision is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will

File No. A-93-341
Page 4


occur.  Certainty is not required.  However, if an effect is only
a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Mr. Cannon has estimated that
the property would increase in value by approximately $30,000 if a
fire flow certificate is issued and the property could be
developed.  Therefore, the effect is foreseeable.

Materiality

     The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the effect of a decision is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision. In this situation,
Mr. Cannon's interest in the real property involved in the decision
pursuant to Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) and the effect of the
moratorium decision is deemed material since:

          (B) The decision involves the issuance,
          denial or revocation of a license, permit
          or other land use entitlement authorizing
          a specific use or uses of such property.

                         Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(B).

     If the district lifts the moratorium on the issuance and
approval of fire flow certificates, a building permit could be
issued and the property could be developed. Without this
certificate, the property may not be developed. Therefore, Mr.
Cannon may not participate in the district decision to lift the
moratorium on the issuance of fire flow certificates.

Public Generally

     You have also inquired if the "public generally" exception²
applies to Mr. Cannon. For the "public generally" exception to
apply, Regulation 18703 requires that the government decision
affects the official's economic interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect the economic interests of a significant
segment of the public. Your facts indicate that this decision
will affect a significant segment of the public (the decision
affects all property owners since a fire flow certificate is
necessary to obtain a building permit).

     However, it appears that this decision would not affect
Mr. Cannon in substantially the same manner as the other property
owners in the district. Mr. Cannon is a partial owner of a five
acre parcel of property he also has an ownership interest in one
of the thirty-three vacant lots in the district which are served
by water meters. Since the property is currently served by a
water meter, it could be developed if the moratorium on the fire

2
     Enclosed is a copy of Regulation 18703. The former version of
this regulation was repealed and the new version was adopted by
the Commission on September 2, 1993.

File No. A-93-341
Page 5


flow certificates is lifted. The majority of lots with
development potential are not currently served by a water meter
and, therefore, face greater impediments to development since
there is also a moratorium on the issuance of new water meter
connections.

The effect of this decision would permit Mr. Cannon and his
partners to develop their property. Many of the other properties
in the district are already developed and would not be affected by
the decision. You indicated that 405 of the 420 residences in the
district are connected to water meters. Accordingly, it does not
appear that the "public generally" exception applies to
Mr. Cannon; he may not participate in the decision to lift the
fire flow certificate moratorium.

I trust this answers your questions.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jim Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enc.
